---
title: "DuckDB List Column Migration Guide"
subtitle: "Step-by-step guide for migrating existing code to handle list columns correctly in DuckDB"
category: "migration"
date-created: "2025-08-27"
date-modified: "2025-08-27"
author: "Claude (Principle Revisor)"
type: "guide"
related_to:
  - "DM_R024: List Column Handling Rule"
  - "DM_R025: Type Conversion Between R and DuckDB"
  - "DM_R026: JSON Serialization Strategy"
  - "CH17: DuckDB Database Specifications"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: true
    number-sections: true
---

# Migration Guide: DuckDB List Column Handling {#overview}

This guide helps you migrate existing code that may fail when writing R data frames with list columns to DuckDB.

:::{.callout-warning}
## Breaking Change
As of DuckDB 1.0+, R list columns cannot be directly written to DuckDB tables. This requires code changes in all ETL pipelines handling nested data.
:::

# Part 1: Identifying Affected Code {#identification}

## Section 1: Symptoms of the Problem {#symptoms}

Your code is affected if you see any of these errors:

```
Error: Can't convert <list> to <character>
Error: rapi_prepare: Failed to prepare query SELECT * FROM data_frame
Error: Unable to transform to native DuckDB type
```

## Section 2: Finding Affected Code {#finding-code}

Search your codebase for patterns that may be affected:

```r
# Search for potential issues in your R scripts
search_patterns <- c(
  "dbWriteTable.*conn",           # Direct writes to DuckDB
  "fromJSON.*flatten",            # JSON parsing that creates lists
  "unnest",                       # May indicate nested data
  "I\\(list\\(",                  # Explicit list columns
  "line_items",                   # Common nested field name
  "metadata",                     # Often contains nested data
  "_raw"                          # Raw data tables often have lists
)

# Function to scan files
scan_for_issues <- function(directory = "scripts/") {
  files <- list.files(directory, pattern = "\\.R$", 
                      recursive = TRUE, full.names = TRUE)
  
  issues <- list()
  for (file in files) {
    content <- readLines(file)
    for (pattern in search_patterns) {
      matches <- grep(pattern, content, value = TRUE)
      if (length(matches) > 0) {
        issues[[length(issues) + 1]] <- list(
          file = file,
          pattern = pattern,
          lines = matches
        )
      }
    }
  }
  return(issues)
}
```

# Part 2: Migration Strategies {#strategies}

## Section 1: Quick Fix - Wrapper Function {#quick-fix}

The fastest migration path is to create a wrapper function:

### Step 1: Create the Wrapper

```r
# Save this in scripts/global_scripts/01_db/dbWriteTableSafe.R

#' Safe wrapper for dbWriteTable that handles list columns
#' @export
dbWriteTableSafe <- function(conn, name, value, ...) {
  # Check for list columns
  list_cols <- sapply(value, function(x) is.list(x) && !is.data.frame(x))
  
  if (any(list_cols)) {
    message("Converting list columns for DuckDB compatibility...")
    value_safe <- value
    
    for (col in names(value_safe)[list_cols]) {
      # Convert to JSON
      value_safe[[paste0(col, "_json")]] <- sapply(
        value_safe[[col]], 
        jsonlite::toJSON, 
        auto_unbox = TRUE,
        null = "null"
      )
      # Remove original column
      value_safe[[col]] <- NULL
      message(sprintf("  - Converted '%s' to '%s_json'", col, col))
    }
    
    dbWriteTable(conn, name, value_safe, ...)
  } else {
    dbWriteTable(conn, name, value, ...)
  }
}
```

### Step 2: Replace All Calls

```r
# Before
dbWriteTable(conn, "my_table", df_with_lists)

# After
dbWriteTableSafe(conn, "my_table", df_with_lists)
```

## Section 2: Comprehensive Migration {#comprehensive}

For a more thorough solution, implement proper handling at each stage:

### Step 1: Update Import Functions

```r
# Before: Direct import may create list columns
import_api_data <- function(endpoint) {
  response <- httr::GET(endpoint)
  df <- jsonlite::fromJSON(httr::content(response, "text"), 
                           flatten = TRUE)
  return(df)
}

# After: Handle list columns immediately
import_api_data <- function(endpoint) {
  response <- httr::GET(endpoint)
  df <- jsonlite::fromJSON(httr::content(response, "text"), 
                           flatten = TRUE)
  
  # Handle list columns
  df <- prepare_for_duckdb(df)
  
  return(df)
}

prepare_for_duckdb <- function(df) {
  list_cols <- sapply(df, function(x) is.list(x) && !is.data.frame(x))
  
  for (col in names(df)[list_cols]) {
    # Decide based on content
    if (is_regular_nested_structure(df[[col]])) {
      # Can flatten
      df <- tidyr::unnest(df, all_of(col), keep_empty = TRUE)
    } else {
      # Must serialize
      df[[paste0(col, "_json")]] <- sapply(df[[col]], 
                                           jsonlite::toJSON, 
                                           auto_unbox = TRUE)
      df[[col]] <- NULL
    }
  }
  
  return(df)
}

is_regular_nested_structure <- function(list_col) {
  # Check if all elements have same structure
  if (length(list_col) == 0) return(FALSE)
  
  # Get structure of first non-null element
  first_struct <- NULL
  for (elem in list_col) {
    if (!is.null(elem)) {
      first_struct <- names(elem)
      break
    }
  }
  
  if (is.null(first_struct)) return(FALSE)
  
  # Check if all elements match
  for (elem in list_col) {
    if (!is.null(elem) && !identical(names(elem), first_struct)) {
      return(FALSE)
    }
  }
  
  return(TRUE)
}
```

### Step 2: Update ETL Scripts

Example migration for Cyberbiz ETL:

```r
# BEFORE: cbz_ETL01_0IM.R (causes errors)
df_orders_raw <- fetch_all_pages("/orders")
if (nrow(df_orders_raw) > 0) {
  # This fails if line_items is a list column!
  dbWriteTable(raw_data, "df_cbz_orders_raw", 
               df_orders_raw, overwrite = TRUE)
}

# AFTER: cbz_ETL01_0IM.R (handles list columns)
df_orders_raw <- fetch_all_pages("/orders")
if (nrow(df_orders_raw) > 0) {
  # Check for line_items list column
  if ("line_items" %in% names(df_orders_raw) && 
      is.list(df_orders_raw$line_items)) {
    
    # Option 1: Flatten if you need the details
    df_sales_raw <- df_orders_raw %>%
      tidyr::unnest(line_items, keep_empty = TRUE)
    dbWriteTable(raw_data, "df_cbz_sales_raw", 
                 df_sales_raw, overwrite = TRUE)
    
    # Option 2: Keep orders with JSON line items
    df_orders_raw$line_items_json <- sapply(
      df_orders_raw$line_items,
      jsonlite::toJSON,
      auto_unbox = TRUE
    )
    df_orders_raw$line_items <- NULL
    dbWriteTable(raw_data, "df_cbz_orders_raw", 
                 df_orders_raw, overwrite = TRUE)
  } else {
    # No list columns, safe to write
    dbWriteTable(raw_data, "df_cbz_orders_raw", 
                 df_orders_raw, overwrite = TRUE)
  }
}
```

# Part 3: Testing Your Migration {#testing}

## Section 1: Unit Tests {#unit-tests}

Create tests to verify your migration:

```r
# tests/test_duckdb_compatibility.R

test_that("List columns are handled correctly", {
  # Create test data with list column
  test_df <- data.frame(
    id = 1:3,
    name = c("A", "B", "C"),
    data = I(list(
      c(1, 2, 3),
      c(4, 5),
      c(6, 7, 8, 9)
    ))
  )
  
  # Setup temp database
  conn <- dbConnect(duckdb(), ":memory:")
  on.exit(dbDisconnect(conn))
  
  # Should not error
  expect_no_error({
    dbWriteTableSafe(conn, "test_table", test_df)
  })
  
  # Verify data was written
  result <- dbGetQuery(conn, "SELECT * FROM test_table")
  expect_equal(nrow(result), 3)
  expect_true("data_json" %in% names(result))
  
  # Verify JSON can be parsed back
  parsed_data <- lapply(result$data_json, jsonlite::fromJSON)
  expect_equal(parsed_data[[1]], c(1, 2, 3))
})
```

## Section 2: Integration Tests {#integration-tests}

Test with real API data:

```r
test_that("API import handles nested data", {
  # Mock API response with nested structure
  mock_response <- '[
    {
      "id": 1,
      "customer": "John",
      "line_items": [
        {"product": "A", "qty": 2},
        {"product": "B", "qty": 1}
      ]
    }
  ]'
  
  # Parse as if from API
  df <- jsonlite::fromJSON(mock_response, flatten = FALSE)
  
  # Apply migration
  df_safe <- prepare_for_duckdb(df)
  
  # Should be writable to DuckDB
  conn <- dbConnect(duckdb(), ":memory:")
  on.exit(dbDisconnect(conn))
  
  expect_no_error({
    dbWriteTable(conn, "orders", df_safe)
  })
})
```

# Part 4: Rollback Plan {#rollback}

## Section 1: Keeping Backward Compatibility {#backward-compat}

If you need to maintain compatibility with existing code:

```r
# Create a compatibility layer
dbWriteTableCompat <- function(conn, name, value, 
                                use_safe = getOption("duckdb.safe_write", TRUE), 
                                ...) {
  if (use_safe) {
    dbWriteTableSafe(conn, name, value, ...)
  } else {
    # Try unsafe first, fall back to safe on error
    tryCatch({
      dbWriteTable(conn, name, value, ...)
    }, error = function(e) {
      if (grepl("Can't convert.*list", e$message)) {
        warning("List column detected, using safe write")
        dbWriteTableSafe(conn, name, value, ...)
      } else {
        stop(e)
      }
    })
  }
}
```

## Section 2: Reverting Changes {#reverting}

If you need to revert:

1. **Keep original data**: Store both JSON and original format during transition
2. **Use different table names**: Append `_v2` to migrated tables
3. **Dual-write period**: Write to both old and new format temporarily

```r
# Transition period dual-write
write_with_transition <- function(conn, name, value) {
  # Try old way (may fail)
  tryCatch({
    dbWriteTable(conn, paste0(name, "_old"), value, overwrite = TRUE)
  }, error = function(e) {
    warning("Old format failed: ", e$message)
  })
  
  # Always write new way
  dbWriteTableSafe(conn, paste0(name, "_v2"), value, overwrite = TRUE)
}
```

# Part 5: Checklist {#checklist}

## Migration Checklist

Use this checklist for each affected script:

### Pre-Migration
- [ ] Identify all `dbWriteTable` calls
- [ ] Check for API imports using `fromJSON`
- [ ] Review data structures for list columns
- [ ] Create backups of existing data

### Implementation
- [ ] Add `dbWriteTableSafe` wrapper function
- [ ] Update ETL import scripts
- [ ] Handle nested structures appropriately
- [ ] Add type checking before writes

### Testing
- [ ] Test with sample data containing lists
- [ ] Test with empty/NULL values
- [ ] Test with production-like data volumes
- [ ] Verify JSON columns can be queried

### Deployment
- [ ] Update documentation
- [ ] Communicate JSON column names to team
- [ ] Monitor for errors in production
- [ ] Plan for data recovery if needed

### Post-Migration
- [ ] Remove old compatibility code
- [ ] Update schema documentation
- [ ] Document JSON column structures
- [ ] Create helper functions for JSON access

# Part 6: Common Patterns {#patterns}

## Pattern 1: API Response with Line Items

```r
# Common e-commerce pattern
handle_order_with_items <- function(order_data) {
  if ("line_items" %in% names(order_data)) {
    # Flatten to sales records
    sales_data <- order_data %>%
      tidyr::unnest(line_items, keep_empty = TRUE)
    
    # Remove the nested column from orders
    order_data$line_items <- NULL
    
    return(list(
      orders = order_data,
      sales = sales_data
    ))
  }
  return(list(orders = order_data))
}
```

## Pattern 2: Metadata Fields

```r
# Common pattern for flexible metadata
handle_metadata <- function(df) {
  if ("metadata" %in% names(df) && is.list(df$metadata)) {
    # Keep as JSON for flexibility
    df$metadata_json <- sapply(df$metadata, 
                               jsonlite::toJSON, 
                               auto_unbox = TRUE)
    df$metadata <- NULL
  }
  return(df)
}
```

## Pattern 3: Array Fields

```r
# Common pattern for arrays (tags, categories, etc.)
handle_arrays <- function(df) {
  array_cols <- c("tags", "categories", "images")
  
  for (col in array_cols) {
    if (col %in% names(df) && is.list(df[[col]])) {
      # For simple arrays, could join to string
      if (all(sapply(df[[col]], function(x) 
                     is.null(x) || is.atomic(x)))) {
        df[[col]] <- sapply(df[[col]], function(x) 
                           paste(x, collapse = ","))
      } else {
        # Complex arrays need JSON
        df[[paste0(col, "_json")]] <- sapply(df[[col]], 
                                             jsonlite::toJSON, 
                                             auto_unbox = TRUE)
        df[[col]] <- NULL
      }
    }
  }
  return(df)
}
```

---

*Migration Guide Version: 1.0*
*For: DuckDB 1.0+ with R DBI interface*
*Critical for: All ETL pipelines handling nested data structures*
